title: Leg Ulcer — Venous vs Arterial
oneLiner: Chronic lower limb ulcer; venous (80%) vs arterial (20%); key to avoid amputation by differentiating management – compression vs revascularisation.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Necrotising fasciitis / deep infection** → rapidly spreading erythema, crepitus, systemic sepsis, severe pain out of proportion to exam → immediate senior review, blood cultures, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, urgent surgical debridement.
- **Acute limb ischaemia** (on background of chronic arterial ulcer) → sudden onset of severe pain, pallor, pulselessness, perishing cold, paralysis → immediate vascular consult, ABCDE, consider heparin, CT angiogram.
- **Critically ischaemic limb with rest pain** → ABPI <0.4, no audible doppler signal, or toe pressure <30 mmHg → urgent vascular referral; risk of gangrene.
sections.Presentation:
- **Venous ulcer** (the "common" one):
  - Usually gaiter area (medial malleolus), shallow, moist, irregular border.
  - Surrounding skin: lipodermatosclerosis (firm, brown induration), haemosiderin staining, varicose veins, oedema.
  - Pain: mild to moderate, worse on dependency, better with elevation.
- **Arterial ulcer**:
  - Distal (toes, heel, lateral malleolus), deep, punched-out, pale/necrotic base, dry.
  - Skin: shiny, hairless, cold, pallor on elevation, dependent rubor.
  - Pain: severe, constant, worse at night, improved by hanging leg down.
- **Mixed aetiology** (common in elderly): features of both + ABPI 0.5-0.8; requires expert wound care.
- **Key exam findings**:
  - Palpate all pulses (femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial).
  - Doppler ankle pressures (handheld doppler) – if absent, suspect arterial.
  - Measure ABPI (ankle-brachial pressure index) – but remember calcified vessels (diabetes, CKD) can give falsely high ABPI; use toe pressure if needed.
  - Buerger’s test: elevate legs – pallor in arterial disease; then lower – dependent rubor.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Venous ulcer** (80%) → ABPI >0.8, venous reflux on duplex, typical location.
- **Arterial ulcer** (20%) → ABPI <0.5 (or <0.9 with symptoms), absent pulses, claudication history.
- **Mixed ulcer** → ABPI 0.5-0.8, features of both.
- **Diabetic foot ulcer** → neuropathic (painless, at pressure sites) +/- ischaemic; check monofilament + doppler.
- **Pressure ulcer** (sacrum, heel) → immobility, shearing forces, Braden score ≤18.
- **Vasculitic ulcer** (rheumatoid, lupus, livedoid vasculopathy) → painful, purpuric, livedo racemosa; check CRP/ESR, ANA, ANCA, biopsy.
- **Malignant ulcer** (SCC, BCC, Marjolin’s) → rolled edges, chronic non-healing, suspicious appearance → biopsy needed.
- **Infection / osteomyelitis** → purulence, probe-to-bone test, XR, MRI, bone biopsy.
- **Pyoderma gangrenosum** → rapidly expanding, painful, undermined violaceous edge; associated with IBD; biopsy shows neutrophilic infiltrate, treat with steroids.
- **Splitter:** "ABPI <0.5 + absent pulses" → arterial (do NOT compress); "ABPI >0.8 + varicose veins + lipodermatosclerosis" → venous (DO compress).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Handheld doppler to measure ABPI (first step).
  - Buerger’s test.
  - Wound swab only if clinical infection (cellulitis, purulence) – not for routine colonisation.
  - Probe-to-bone test if suspicious of osteomyelitis (diabetic foot, deep ulcer).
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR (to rule out infection, vasculitis).
  - U&Es, HbA1c (diabetes screening).
  - ANA, ANCA, cryoglobulins if vasculitis suspected.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Venous duplex ultrasound (for venous reflux/obstruction) + arterial duplex (if ABPI <0.9 or abnormal pulses).
  - **Second-line:** Angiography (CT/MR/DSA) if revascularisation considered.
  - **Plain XR** (foot/ankle) if osteomyelitis suspected.
- **Special tests:**
  - Toe brachial pressure index (TBPI) – for calcified arteries (diabetes, CKD) – normal >0.7; critical ischaemia <0.3.
  - Skin biopsy (if atypical, suspected vasculitis, malignancy).
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **CEAP classification (Clinical, Etiological, Anatomical, Pathophysiological)** for venous disease: C0–C6 (C6 = active ulcer).
  - **Rutherford classification** for arterial ischaemia: grades 0–6; grade 4 = rest pain, grade 5/6 = ulcer/gangrene.
- **Never-miss test:** **Doppler ABPI** (handheld) in EVERY leg ulcer – omission leads to wrong compression therapy and limb loss.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Assess ABCDE** if septic or acute ischaemia.
  - **Pain control** – paracetamol first-line; consider opioids for severe rest pain.
  - **Elevate leg** if venous; **hang leg down** if arterial rest pain.
  - **Wound care:**
    - Clean with saline; remove slough if needed (debridement).
    - Apply non-adherent dressing (e.g., silicone).
  - **Infection:** if cellulitis (erythema, warmth, pus, fever) → start empirical antibiotics per local policy (usually flucloxacillin + or metronidazole if anaerobic; or co-amoxiclav).
  - **Arterial:** urgent vascular referral if ABPI <0.4, rest pain, or acute ischaemia.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Confirm aetiology** – complete ABPI, duplex, specialist review.
  - **Venous ulcer:**
    - **Compression therapy** – multi-layer bandaging (e.g., 4-layer system) OR hosiery (if ABPI >0.8). Start with low pressure if mixed aetiology. **NEVER compress if ABPI <0.5**.
    - Leg elevation above heart when resting.
    - Regular mobilisation + ankle exercises.
  - **Arterial ulcer:**
    - **Vascular review** – consider revascularisation (angioplasty/bypass).
    - **Pain management** – gabapentin/pregabalin for neuropathic rest pain (check renal function).
    - **Wound care** – keep dry, avoid infection; debridement only by specialist.
  - **Mixed ulcer:** compression only under vascular/leg ulcer clinic guidance.
  - **Optimise comorbidities:** diabetes control, stop smoking, treat hypertension, antiplatelet (aspirin/clopidogrel for arterial).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Venous:** lifelong compression hosiery (class 2 or 3), leg elevation, weight loss, varicose vein treatment (surgery/endovenous) if incompetent saphenous.
  - **Arterial:** secondary prevention (antiplatelet, statin), supervised exercise programme, smoking cessation.
  - **Wound follow-up** – district nurse 2-3 times/week for dressing changes.
  - **Healing time** – venous: 6-12 weeks with compression; arterial: variable, may not heal without revascularisation.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% sodium chloride if septic/NBM; otherwise oral hydration encouraged.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., dalteparin 5,000 units SC OD) unless contraindicated or high bleeding risk.
  - **Diet/NBM:** normal diet; consider nutritional supplements (e.g., high-protein, zinc, vitamin C) if malnourished.
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS2 4-hourly (more if unstable).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Venous:** no specific drug; compression is mainstay.
  - **Arterial:** antiplatelet – **aspirin 75mg OD** PO (if not contraindicated); or **clopidogrel 75mg OD** if aspirin intolerant.
  - **Statin:** **atorvastatin 20–80mg OD** (target LDL <1.8 mmol/L).
  - **Pain:** **paracetamol 1g QDS** PO; **gabapentin** (start 300mg OD, titrate up to 300–600mg TDS; check renal dose) or **pregabalin 75–150mg BD** (renal adjust).
  - **Infection (cellulitis):** **flucloxacillin 500mg QDS PO** for 7 days; if penicillin allergic – **clindamycin 300-450mg QDS** or **doxycycline 100mg BD**. (For IV: flucloxacillin 1g QDS IV; if severe sepsis, add metronidazole or clindamycin per local policy.)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Pentoxifylline 400mg TDS** – used as adjuvant for venous ulcers (weak evidence; can cause GI upset).
  - **Rivaroxaban 15mg BD** (for venous thromboembolism prophylaxis in high-risk patients, but not routine for ulcers).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Flucloxacillin:** avoid in penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis); caution in hepatic impairment.
  - **Aspirin:** avoid in active peptic ulcer, bleeding disorder; caution with NSAIDs.
  - **Gabapentin/pregabalin:** cause dizziness, sedation; avoid in severe renal impairment (reduce dose).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Gabapentin:** reduce dose if eGFR <30 (e.g., 300mg QD or 300mg QOD).
  - **Pregabalin:** adjust based on eGFR.
  - **Atorvastatin:** caution in severe hepatic impairment (reduced dose).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Applying compression in unrecognised arterial disease (ABPI <0.5)** – can cause rapid worsening of ischaemia, necrosis, and limb loss. *Always check ABPI before compression.*
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Cellulitis / sepsis** – progressing from infected ulcer.
  - **Deep vein thrombosis** (venous stasis) – if compression not applied correctly.
  - **Acute limb ischaemia** (arterial) – plaque rupture, thrombosis.
  - **Compression injury** – skin necrosis due to excessive pressure.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Venous:** lipodermatosclerosis, atrophie blanche, chronic oedema, cellulitis recurrence, malignant transformation (Marjolin’s ulcer – SCC).
  - **Arterial:** rest pain, claudication, critical limb ischaemia, gangrene, amputation.
sections.Prognosis:
- **Venous:** high healing rate (70-90% in 12 weeks) with compression; recurrence up to 70% without lifelong hosiery.
- **Arterial:** poor healing without revascularisation; 1-year major amputation risk ~15% in critical ischaemia; mortality increased (all-cause) due to cardiovascular disease.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **ALWAYS measure ABPI before applying compression** – hand-held doppler is cheap and life-saving.
- **In infected ulcers, take a wound swab** (microscopy, culture and sensitivity) before starting antibiotics – but don’t delay antibiotics if systemic signs.
- **Refer all atypical ulcers** (location, shape, bilateral, non-healing despite 3 months of standard care) for vascular/ dermatology opinion and possible biopsy.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Compressing without ABPI** – can convert a manageable ulcer into a necrotic limb.
- **Treating colonised ulcers with systemic antibiotics** – overuse promotes resistance; only treat clinical infection.
- **Ignoring smoker’s pack years** – smoking is the single most modifiable risk factor for arterial disease; offer smoking cessation (NRT, varenicline, referral).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening pain (especially resting or at night), spreading redness/cellulitis, fever/chills, new colour changes in foot (pallor, blue, black), sudden inability to walk, or any fresh oozing/pus.
- **Follow-up:** GP within 1 week for wound review; vascular clinic within 2 weeks if ABPI <0.9; leg ulcer nurse specialist (if available) for ongoing care.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / medical team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or trending upwards.
  - New severe rest pain or acute limb changes (pale, cold, no pulses).
  - ABPI <0.4 or drop >0.15 from baseline.
  - Suspected critical limb ischaemia (rest pain, ulcer depth, gangrene).
  - Suspected necrotising fasciitis (crepitus, systemic toxicity).
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - Sepsis with persistent hypotension after fluids (MAP <65).
  - Acute limb ischaemia with extensive motor deficit (fixed toes, paralysis).

rawMarkdown:
## Leg Ulcer — Venous vs Arterial
**One-liner:** Chronic lower limb ulcer; venous (80%) vs arterial (20%); key to avoid amputation by differentiating management – compression vs revascularisation.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Necrotising fasciitis / deep infection** → rapidly spreading erythema, crepitus, systemic sepsis, severe pain out of proportion to exam → immediate senior review, blood cultures, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, urgent surgical debridement.
- **Acute limb ischaemia** (on background of chronic arterial ulcer) → sudden onset of severe pain, pallor, pulselessness, perishing cold, paralysis → immediate vascular consult, ABCDE, consider heparin, CT angiogram.
- **Critically ischaemic limb with rest pain** → ABPI <0.4, no audible doppler signal, or toe pressure <30 mmHg → urgent vascular referral; risk of gangrene.

### Presentation
- **Venous ulcer** (the "common" one):
  - Usually gaiter area (medial malleolus), shallow, moist, irregular border.
  - Surrounding skin: lipodermatosclerosis (firm, brown induration), haemosiderin staining, varicose veins, oedema.
  - Pain: mild to moderate, worse on dependency, better with elevation.
- **Arterial ulcer**:
  - Distal (toes, heel, lateral malleolus), deep, punched-out, pale/necrotic base, dry.
  - Skin: shiny, hairless, cold, pallor on elevation, dependent rubor.
  - Pain: severe, constant, worse at night, improved by hanging leg down.
- **Mixed aetiology** (common in elderly): features of both + ABPI 0.5-0.8; requires expert wound care.
- **Key exam findings**:
  - Palpate all pulses (femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial).
  - Doppler ankle pressures (handheld doppler) – if absent, suspect arterial.
  - Measure ABPI (ankle-brachial pressure index) – but remember calcified vessels (diabetes, CKD) can give falsely high ABPI; use toe pressure if needed.
  - Buerger’s test: elevate legs – pallor in arterial disease; then lower – dependent rubor.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Venous ulcer** (80%) → ABPI >0.8, venous reflux on duplex, typical location.
- **Arterial ulcer** (20%) → ABPI <0.5 (or <0.9 with symptoms), absent pulses, claudication history.
- **Mixed ulcer** → ABPI 0.5-0.8, features of both.
- **Diabetic foot ulcer** → neuropathic (painless, at pressure sites) +/- ischaemic; check monofilament + doppler.
- **Pressure ulcer** (sacrum, heel) → immobility, shearing forces, Braden score ≤18.
- **Vasculitic ulcer** (rheumatoid, lupus, livedoid vasculopathy) → painful, purpuric, livedo racemosa; check CRP/ESR, ANA, ANCA, biopsy.
- **Malignant ulcer** (SCC, BCC, Marjolin’s) → rolled edges, chronic non-healing, suspicious appearance → biopsy needed.
- **Infection / osteomyelitis** → purulence, probe-to-bone test, XR, MRI, bone biopsy.
- **Pyoderma gangrenosum** → rapidly expanding, painful, undermined violaceous edge; associated with IBD; biopsy shows neutrophilic infiltrate, treat with steroids.
- **Splitter:** "ABPI <0.5 + absent pulses" → arterial (do NOT compress); "ABPI >0.8 + varicose veins + lipodermatosclerosis" → venous (DO compress).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Handheld doppler to measure ABPI (first step).
  - Buerger’s test.
  - Wound swab only if clinical infection (cellulitis, purulence) – not for routine colonisation.
  - Probe-to-bone test if suspicious of osteomyelitis (diabetic foot, deep ulcer).
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR (to rule out infection, vasculitis).
  - U&Es, HbA1c (diabetes screening).
  - ANA, ANCA, cryoglobulins if vasculitis suspected.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Venous duplex ultrasound (for venous reflux/obstruction) + arterial duplex (if ABPI <0.9 or abnormal pulses).
  - **Second-line:** Angiography (CT/MR/DSA) if revascularisation considered.
  - **Plain XR** (foot/ankle) if osteomyelitis suspected.
- **Special tests:**
  - Toe brachial pressure index (TBPI) – for calcified arteries (diabetes, CKD) – normal >0.7; critical ischaemia <0.3.
  - Skin biopsy (if atypical, suspected vasculitis, malignancy).
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **CEAP classification (Clinical, Etiological, Anatomical, Pathophysiological)** for venous disease: C0–C6 (C6 = active ulcer).
  - **Rutherford classification** for arterial ischaemia: grades 0–6; grade 4 = rest pain, grade 5/6 = ulcer/gangrene.
- **Never-miss test:** **Doppler ABPI** (handheld) in EVERY leg ulcer – omission leads to wrong compression therapy and limb loss.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Assess ABCDE** if septic or acute ischaemia.
  - **Pain control** – paracetamol first-line; consider opioids for severe rest pain.
  - **Elevate leg** if venous; **hang leg down** if arterial rest pain.
  - **Wound care:**
    - Clean with saline; remove slough if needed (debridement).
    - Apply non-adherent dressing (e.g., silicone).
  - **Infection:** if cellulitis (erythema, warmth, pus, fever) → start empirical antibiotics per local policy (usually flucloxacillin + or metronidazole if anaerobic; or co-amoxiclav).
  - **Arterial:** urgent vascular referral if ABPI <0.4, rest pain, or acute ischaemia.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Confirm aetiology** – complete ABPI, duplex, specialist review.
  - **Venous ulcer:**
    - **Compression therapy** – multi-layer bandaging (e.g., 4-layer system) OR hosiery (if ABPI >0.8). Start with low pressure if mixed aetiology. **NEVER compress if ABPI <0.5**.
    - Leg elevation above heart when resting.
    - Regular mobilisation + ankle exercises.
  - **Arterial ulcer:**
    - **Vascular review** – consider revascularisation (angioplasty/bypass).
    - **Pain management** – gabapentin/pregabalin for neuropathic rest pain (check renal function).
    - **Wound care** – keep dry, avoid infection; debridement only by specialist.
  - **Mixed ulcer:** compression only under vascular/leg ulcer clinic guidance.
  - **Optimise comorbidities:** diabetes control, stop smoking, treat hypertension, antiplatelet (aspirin/clopidogrel for arterial).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Venous:** lifelong compression hosiery (class 2 or 3), leg elevation, weight loss, varicose vein treatment (surgery/endovenous) if incompetent saphenous.
  - **Arterial:** secondary prevention (antiplatelet, statin), supervised exercise programme, smoking cessation.
  - **Wound follow-up** – district nurse 2-3 times/week for dressing changes.
  - **Healing time** – venous: 6-12 weeks with compression; arterial: variable, may not heal without revascularisation.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% sodium chloride if septic/NBM; otherwise oral hydration encouraged.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., dalteparin 5,000 units SC OD) unless contraindicated or high bleeding risk.
  - **Diet/NBM:** normal diet; consider nutritional supplements (e.g., high-protein, zinc, vitamin C) if malnourished.
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS2 4-hourly (more if unstable).

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Venous:** no specific drug; compression is mainstay.
  - **Arterial:** antiplatelet – **aspirin 75mg OD** PO (if not contraindicated); or **clopidogrel 75mg OD** if aspirin intolerant.
  - **Statin:** **atorvastatin 20–80mg OD** (target LDL <1.8 mmol/L).
  - **Pain:** **paracetamol 1g QDS** PO; **gabapentin** (start 300mg OD, titrate up to 300–600mg TDS; check renal dose) or **pregabalin 75–150mg BD** (renal adjust).
  - **Infection (cellulitis):** **flucloxacillin 500mg QDS PO** for 7 days; if penicillin allergic – **clindamycin 300-450mg QDS** or **doxycycline 100mg BD**. (For IV: flucloxacillin 1g QDS IV; if severe sepsis, add metronidazole or clindamycin per local policy.)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Pentoxifylline 400mg TDS** – used as adjuvant for venous ulcers (weak evidence; can cause GI upset).
  - **Rivaroxaban 15mg BD** (for venous thromboembolism prophylaxis in high-risk patients, but not routine for ulcers).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Flucloxacillin:** avoid in penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis); caution in hepatic impairment.
  - **Aspirin:** avoid in active peptic ulcer, bleeding disorder; caution with NSAIDs.
  - **Gabapentin/pregabalin:** cause dizziness, sedation; avoid in severe renal impairment (reduce dose).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Gabapentin:** reduce dose if eGFR <30 (e.g., 300mg QD or 300mg QOD).
  - **Pregabalin:** adjust based on eGFR.
  - **Atorvastatin:** caution in severe hepatic impairment (reduced dose).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Applying compression in unrecognised arterial disease (ABPI <0.5)** – can cause rapid worsening of ischaemia, necrosis, and limb loss. *Always check ABPI before compression.*

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Cellulitis / sepsis** – progressing from infected ulcer.
  - **Deep vein thrombosis** (venous stasis) – if compression not applied correctly.
  - **Acute limb ischaemia** (arterial) – plaque rupture, thrombosis.
  - **Compression injury** – skin necrosis due to excessive pressure.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Venous:** lipodermatosclerosis, atrophie blanche, chronic oedema, cellulitis recurrence, malignant transformation (Marjolin’s ulcer – SCC).
  - **Arterial:** rest pain, claudication, critical limb ischaemia, gangrene, amputation.

### Prognosis
- **Venous:** high healing rate (70-90% in 12 weeks) with compression; recurrence up to 70% without lifelong hosiery.
- **Arterial:** poor healing without revascularisation; 1-year major amputation risk ~15% in critical ischaemia; mortality increased (all-cause) due to cardiovascular disease.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **ALWAYS measure ABPI before applying compression** – hand-held doppler is cheap and life-saving.
- **In infected ulcers, take a wound swab** (microscopy, culture and sensitivity) before starting antibiotics – but don’t delay antibiotics if systemic signs.
- **Refer all atypical ulcers** (location, shape, bilateral, non-healing despite 3 months of standard care) for vascular/ dermatology opinion and possible biopsy.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Compressing without ABPI** – can convert a manageable ulcer into a necrotic limb.
- **Treating colonised ulcers with systemic antibiotics** – overuse promotes resistance; only treat clinical infection.
- **Ignoring smoker’s pack years** – smoking is the single most modifiable risk factor for arterial disease; offer smoking cessation (NRT, varenicline, referral).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening pain (especially resting or at night), spreading redness/cellulitis, fever/chills, new colour changes in foot (pallor, blue, black), sudden inability to walk, or any fresh oozing/pus.
- **Follow-up:** GP within 1 week for wound review; vascular clinic within 2 weeks if ABPI <0.9; leg ulcer nurse specialist (if available) for ongoing care.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / medical team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or trending upwards.
  - New severe rest pain or acute limb changes (pale, cold, no pulses).
  - ABPI <0.4 or drop >0.15 from baseline.
  - Suspected critical limb ischaemia (rest pain, ulcer depth, gangrene).
  - Suspected necrotising fasciitis (crepitus, systemic toxicity).
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - Sepsis with persistent hypotension after fluids (MAP <65).
  - Acute limb ischaemia with extensive motor deficit (fixed toes, paralysis).
